an10.60:0.2: 6. The Chapter on One’s Own Mind
an10.60:1.1: At one time, the _Bhagavā_ was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
an10.60:1.2: Now at that time the venerable Girimānanda was ill, suffering, seriously sick.
an10.60:1.3: Then the venerable Ānanda went to the _Bhagavā_. Having gone to the _Bhagavā_, he bowed down to the _Bhagavā_ and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, the venerable Ānanda said this to the _Bhagavā_:
an10.60:10.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of cessation [*nirodhasaññā*]?
an10.60:10.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:10.3: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishment of all acquisitions, the ending of *taṇhā*, cessation, *nibbāna*.’
an10.60:10.4: This is called the perception of cessation [*nirodhasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:11.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of non-delight in the whole world [*sabbaloke anabhiratasaññā*]?
an10.60:11.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* dwells giving up whatever clingings/feedings [*upādānā*], mental fixations, insistences, and underlying tendencies there are in the world, without clinging/feeding [*anupādiyanto*].
an10.60:11.3: This is called the perception of non-delight in the whole world [*sabbaloke anabhiratasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:12.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of undesirability in all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhāresu anicchāsaññā*]?
an10.60:12.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* is appalled, shrinks away, and is disgusted by all *saṅkhāras*.
an10.60:12.3: This is called the perception of undesirability in all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhāresu anicchāsaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:13.1: And what, Ānanda, is *sati* of breathing in and out [*ānāpānassati*]?
an10.60:13.10: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the mind *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the mind *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.11: He trains thus: ‘Calming the mind *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Calming the mind *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.12: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the mind [*citta*], I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the mind [*citta*], I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.19: He trains thus: ‘Contemplating relinquishment, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Contemplating relinquishment, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, sits down, having folded his legs crosswise, set his body erect, and established *sati* before him.
an10.60:13.20: This is called *sati* of breathing in and out [*ānāpānassati*], Ānanda.
an10.60:13.3: With *sati*, he breathes in; with *sati*, he breathes out.
an10.60:13.4: Breathing in long, he understands: ‘I breathe in long.’ Breathing out long, he understands: ‘I breathe out long.’
an10.60:13.5: Breathing in short, he understands: ‘I breathe in short.’ Breathing out short, he understands: ‘I breathe out short.’
an10.60:13.6: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.7: He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.8: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *pīti*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *pīti*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.9: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *sukha*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *sukha*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:14.1: If you were to recite these ten perceptions to the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda, Ānanda, it is possible that, when the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda hears these ten perceptions, that illness of his would immediately subside.”
an10.60:15.1: Then the venerable Ānanda, having learned these ten perceptions in the presence of the _Bhagavā_, went to the venerable Girimānanda. Having gone to the venerable Girimānanda, he recited these ten perceptions to him.
an10.60:15.2: Then, when the venerable Girimānanda heard the ten perceptions, that illness of his immediately subsided.
an10.60:15.3: And the venerable Girimānanda recovered from that illness. It was in this way that that illness of the venerable Girimānanda was abandoned.
an10.60:15.5: The first is the Chapter on One’s Own Mind.
an10.60:16.4: roots, the gone-forth one, and Giri.
an10.60:2.1: “Bhante, the venerable Girimānanda is ill, suffering, seriously sick.
an10.60:2.2: Sādhu, bhante, may the _Bhagavā_ go to the venerable Girimānanda out of compassion.”
an10.60:2.3: “Ānanda, if you were to recite the ten perceptions to the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda, it is possible that, when the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda hears the ten perceptions, that illness of his would immediately subside.
an10.60:3.2: The perception of impermanence [*aniccasaññā*], the perception of not-self [*anattasaññā*], the perception of the unattractive [*asubhasaññā*], the perception of drawback [*ādīnavasaññā*], the perception of abandoning [*pahānasaññā*], the perception of fading away [*virāgasaññā*], the perception of cessation [*nirodhasaññā*], the perception of non-delight in the whole world [*sabbaloke anabhiratasaññā*], the perception of undesirability in all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhāresu anicchāsaññā*], and *sati* of breathing in and out [*ānāpānassati*].
an10.60:4.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of impermanence [*aniccasaññā*]?
an10.60:4.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:4.3: ‘*rūpa* is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *vedanā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saññā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*].’
an10.60:4.4: Thus he dwells contemplating impermanence [*aniccā*] in these five aggregates subject to clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*].
an10.60:4.5: This is called the perception of impermanence [*aniccasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:5.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of not-self [*anattasaññā*]?
an10.60:5.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:5.3: ‘The eye is not-self [*anattā*], *rūpas* are not-self [*anattā*], the ear is not-self [*anattā*], sounds are not-self [*anattā*], the nose is not-self [*anattā*], smells are not-self [*anattā*], the tongue is not-self [*anattā*], tastes are not-self [*anattā*], the body is not-self [*anattā*], tangibles are not-self [*anattā*], the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*].’
an10.60:5.4: Thus he dwells contemplating not-self [*anattā*] in these six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*].
an10.60:5.5: This is called the perception of not-self [*anattasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:6.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of the unattractive [*asubhasaññā*]?
an10.60:6.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, upward from the soles of the feet and downward from the top of the hair, enclosed by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
an10.60:6.3: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin; flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys; heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs; intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces; bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat; tears, grease, spittle, snot, joint fluid, urine.’
an10.60:6.4: Thus he dwells contemplating the unattractive in this body.
an10.60:6.5: This is called the perception of the unattractive [*asubhasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:7.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of drawback [*ādīnavasaññā*]?
an10.60:7.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:7.3: ‘This body has much *dukkha*, much drawback. In this body various illnesses arise, that is:
an10.60:7.4: eye disease, hearing disease, nose disease, tongue disease, body disease, head disease, ear disease, mouth disease, tooth disease, lip disease, cough, asthma, catarrh, burning, fever, stomach disease, fainting, diarrhea, colic, cholera, leprosy, boils, skin disease, consumption, epilepsy, ringworm, itching, scabies, nail disease, mange, blood disease, bile disease, diabetes, hemorrhoids, boils, fistulas, illnesses arising from bile, illnesses arising from phlegm, illnesses arising from wind, illnesses arising from the combination of the humors, illnesses born of a change in climate, illnesses born of improper behavior, illnesses caused by assault, illnesses born of the fruition of kamma, cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, urination.’
an10.60:7.5: Thus he dwells contemplating drawback in this body.
an10.60:7.6: This is called the perception of drawback [*ādīnavasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:8.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of abandoning [*pahānasaññā*]?
an10.60:8.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* does not tolerate arisen sensual thought [*kāmavitakkaṁ*]; he gives it up, dispels it, brings it to an end, makes it disappear. He does not tolerate arisen ill-will thought [*byāpādavitakkaṁ*]; he gives it up, dispels it, brings it to an end, makes it disappear. He does not tolerate arisen harming thought [*vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*]; he gives it up, dispels it, brings it to an end, makes it disappear. Whatever bad, unskillful *dhammas* arise, he does not tolerate them; he gives them up, dispels them, brings them to an end, makes them disappear.
an10.60:8.3: This is called the perception of abandoning [*pahānasaññā*], Ānanda.
an10.60:9.1: And what, Ānanda, is the perception of fading away [*virāgasaññā*]?
an10.60:9.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:9.3: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishment of all acquisitions, the ending of *taṇhā*, fading away, *nibbāna*.’
an10.60:9.4: This is called the perception of fading away [*virāgasaññā*], Ānanda.
